title: PFAS Industry Reporting
subtitle: Chemical Disclosure Requirements
description: The regulatory history of mandatory PFAS disclosure — from the first Toxics Release Inventory additions under Trump 1, through Biden's landmark industry-wide data collection rule, to Trump 2's proposed exemptions that would eliminate reporting for millions of products.
currentStatus: Under revision. Reporting window extended to 2026. November 2025 proposed rule would exempt PFAS below 0.1% concentration and in imported articles, reducing industry burden by an estimated 10–11 million hours — and eliminating much of the dataset EPA said it needed to understand PFAS exposure.

timeline.title: Congress mandates PFAS be added to the Toxics Release Inventory
timeline.description: The National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2020, signed by President Trump, requires EPA to add PFAS compounds to the Toxics Release Inventory — the federal database of toxic chemical releases from industrial facilities. The mandate directs EPA to add PFAS that meet certain hazard criteria and to begin collecting release data for calendar year 2020, with reports due in 2021. It is the first federal law requiring systematic public disclosure of industrial PFAS releases.

timeline.title: Trump EPA issues significant new use rule for long-chain PFAS
timeline.description: EPA finalizes a significant new use rule (SNUR) under TSCA requiring companies to notify the agency before manufacturing, importing, or processing certain long-chain PFAS for any use not currently ongoing. The rule creates a review mechanism for new PFAS applications but does not restrict existing uses.

timeline.title: Infrastructure law mandates comprehensive PFAS industry reporting under TSCA
timeline.description: The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (Bipartisan Infrastructure Law) includes a provision directing EPA to require all manufacturers and importers of PFAS — and processors who use PFAS in products — to report data on the chemicals under TSCA Section 8(a)(7). Unlike earlier disclosure requirements, this mandate covers the full universe of PFAS manufacturers regardless of production volume, and applies to historical use going back to 2011. EPA is directed to issue a rule within two years.

timeline.title: Biden EPA proposes comprehensive PFAS data collection rule under TSCA Section 8(a)(7)
timeline.description: EPA publishes a proposed rule that would require all U.S. manufacturers and importers — and processors — of any PFAS compound to report data on production volumes, uses, disposal, exposures, and existing health and environmental studies. The proposal covers more than 1,300 PFAS and applies to all manufacturers that have made or imported PFAS since January 1, 2011. EPA describes it as the most comprehensive PFAS data collection effort in U.S. history.

timeline.title: Biden EPA finalizes TSCA PFAS reporting rule — covering all manufacturers since 2011
timeline.description: EPA publishes the final TSCA Section 8(a)(7) rule requiring all current and past PFAS manufacturers and importers to report data on production, use, disposal, and health and environmental studies. Effective November 2023, with a reporting window opening in November 2024. The rule is described as the most sweeping PFAS data collection ever mandated under U.S. law — EPA expects it to reveal the full scope of industrial PFAS use for the first time.

timeline.title: Trump EPA extends PFAS reporting deadline
timeline.description: The Trump administration announces that the TSCA Section 8(a)(7) reporting window — originally set to open November 12, 2024 and close May 8, 2025 — will be extended. The delay gives the incoming administration time to review the rule before companies submit data. Industry groups had lobbied for additional time to comply with the reporting requirements.

timeline.title: Trump EPA proposes exemptions that would sharply narrow PFAS reporting scope
timeline.description: EPA proposes amendments to the TSCA Section 8(a)(7) rule that would exempt: (1) PFAS present at concentrations below 0.1 percent by weight in a product or process, and (2) PFAS in imported articles. The agency estimates the exemptions would reduce industry compliance burden by 10 to 11 million hours. Critics argue the threshold exemption would eliminate reporting for the vast majority of PFAS in consumer products — precisely the exposure pathway EPA said it needed data on. Environmental groups and state attorneys general warn the change would gut the dataset before it is even collected.

timeline.title: Revised reporting window opens for TSCA Section 8(a)(7)
timeline.description: After multiple extensions, the TSCA PFAS reporting window opens for companies subject to the 2023 rule. The reporting period runs through mid-2026. The scope of who must report — and what they must disclose — remains subject to ongoing litigation and the November 2025 proposed rule, which has not yet been finalized.
